core_meaning: <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> is a remarkably versatile word with three main uses: (1) as an adverb meaning 'quite / fairly / surprisingly' (often implying 'more than expected'), (2) as a な-adjective meaning 'fine / acceptable / sufficient,' and (3) as a polite refusal meaning 'No thank you / I'm good' — this last use trips up many learners because the same word can mean both 'yes, that's fine' and 'no, I don't need it' depending on context.

sentences.jp: この<ruby>映画<rt>えいが</rt></ruby>、<ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby><ruby>面白<rt>おもしろ</rt></ruby>かったじゃん！
sentences.translation: That movie was actually pretty interesting, wasn't it!
sentences.notes: Shows the 'quite / surprisingly so' adverb use — the nuance is mild surprise that it exceeded expectations.

sentences.jp: この<ruby>仕事<rt>しごと</rt></ruby>は<ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby><ruby>難<rt>むずか</rt></ruby>しいから、<ruby>気<rt>き</rt></ruby>をつけてね。
sentences.translation: This job is fairly difficult, so be careful.
sentences.notes: The adverb <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> softens the statement while still conveying that the difficulty level is notable.

sentences.jp: 「もう少しいかがですか？」「いいえ、<ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby>です。」
sentences.translation: 'Would you like a little more?' 'No, I'm fine, thank you.'
sentences.notes: The polite refusal use — this is extremely common in formal or service-industry interactions.

sentences.jp: <ruby>締<rt>し</rt></ruby>め<ruby>切<rt>き</rt></ruby>りまで<ruby>三日<rt>みっか</rt></ruby>あれば<ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby>です。
sentences.translation: Three days until the deadline will be sufficient.
sentences.notes: The な-adjective use meaning 'enough / acceptable' — common in business or polite requests.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>、<ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby><ruby>歌<rt>うた</rt></ruby>うまいよ。<ruby>知<rt>し</rt></ruby>らなかった。
sentences.translation: She's actually a pretty good singer. I had no idea.
sentences.notes: Again the 'more than expected' nuance — <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> here implies pleasant surprise.

dont_use: <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby>です as a refusal can sound abrupt or even rude if the tone isn't warm and polite — in casual settings, <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby> or いいです is more natural for declining. Also avoid using <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> as a strong intensifier like 'very' or 'extremely' — it implies 'quite' or 'more than I expected,' not an extreme degree; use とても or すごく for that. Finally, don't use <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby>です to agree enthusiastically — a listener may misread it as a refusal.

confused_with.contrast: Both <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby>です and <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby>です can refuse an offer, but <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby> is softer and far more common in everyday casual speech — saying 「いや、<ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby>」 when declining a friend's offer feels natural, while 「<ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby>です」 in the same situation might sound stiff or even cold. As an adverb, <ruby>大丈夫<rt>だいじょうぶ</rt></ruby> cannot replace <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> in sentences like <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby><ruby>難<rt>むずか</rt></ruby>しい ('quite difficult').

frequency: <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> is extremely common across all registers — the adverb 'quite' use appears constantly in daily conversation, and the polite refusal use is standard in formal and service contexts. It appears often in both spoken and written Japanese.
anki_hint: <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> has three faces: 'quite' (adverb of degree), 'sufficient/fine' (な-adjective), and 'No thank you' (polite refusal) — the context, especially whether it's accepting or declining something, tells you which meaning applies.
